coredata.dc:title: Amino-embedded carbon quantum dots incorporated thin-film nanocomposite membrane for desalination by pervaporation
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jiawei
Jia, Wei
Guo, Jiaxin
Khanzada, Noman Khalid
Jin, Pengrui
Wong, Pak Wai
Zhang, Xinning
An, Alicia Kyoungjin
coredata.dc:description: This study provides an in-depth investigation on the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in fabricating novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes to address the high demand for highly water-permeable pervaporation (PV) membranes for desalination processes, especially for treating high-salinity water, brine, or wastewater. CQDs have emerged as a green nanofiller for improving membrane performance owing to its excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity, uniform dispersion, and rich functional groups. Herein, we synthesize amino-embedded carbon quantum dots (ACQDs) via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method and employ them in interfacial polymerization to fabricate the ACQD-TFN membrane. Benefiting from the incorporation of ACQDs, the optimized ACQD-TFN membrane exhibited an enhanced water flux of 23.2 kg·m−2·h−1 at 70 °C during the treatment of a 10 wt% NaCl solution, which was 44% higher performance than that of the unmodified thin-film composite membrane. The ACQD-TFN membrane's salt rejection remained at 99.9% even after 96,000 ppm·h chlorine exposure, exhibiting excellent chlorine resistance. Also, the ACQD-TFN membrane's regular surface microstructure and increased hydrophilicity endowed good anti-fouling property, and its flux recovery rate reached 91% after 5 fouling-cleaning cycles. This work provides a feasible strategy for fabricating highly permeable, chlorine-resistant, and anti-fouling PV membranes for desalination applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Interfacial polymerization
Pervaporation
Desalination
Chlorine resistance
Membrane fouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amino-embedded carbon quantum dots (ACQDs)
Characterization of ACQDs and fabricated membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the ACQD-TFN membrane fabrication process.
Schematic diagram of the lab-scale pervaporation setup.

Characterization of the synthesized ACQDs: (a) FTIR spectra of the ACQDs, (b) particle diameter distribution of the ACQDs, (c) UV–vis absorbance spectrum (inset: photos of the ACQD solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light), and (d) fluorescence intensity of the ACQDs at different excitation wavelengths (310–410 nm).

The SEM images of the fabricated membranes: (a-f) surface morphologies of the TFC and ACQD-TFN membranes, (g-i) cross-sectional images of the PES substrate and the TFC and ACQD-TFN (50 wt%) membranes.
The FTIR spectra of the (a) TFC and (b-f) ACQD-TFN membranes (ACQD content from 10, 20, 30, and 40 to 50 wt%).
The water contact angles of the TFC and ACQD-TFN membranes with different ACQD contents (10-50 wt%).

The PV desalination performance of the ACQD-TFN membranes: (a) the effects of ACQD contents (10 wt% NaCl, 70 °C), (b) the long-term PV performance of the ACQD-TFN (50 wt%) membrane (10 wt% NaCl, 70 °C), (c) the effects of feed temperature (10 wt% NaCl, 50–90 °C) and (d) feed concentration (0-20 wt% NaCl, 70 °C) on the ACQD-TFN (50 wt%) membrane.

The chlorine resistance of the ACQD-TFN (50 wt%) membrane: (a) pervaporation flux (initial feed concentration was 10 wt% NaCl, feed temperature was maintained at 70 °C), (b) salt rejection before and after chlorine exposure.
SEM images of the TFC and ACQD-TFN membranes after (a, b) 12 h, and (c, d) 48 h chlorine exposure.
Membrane anti-fouling performance under fouling (2000 ppm HA) and cleaning (DI water) cycle test.
The flux recovery rate of the ACQD-TFN and TFC membranes during the fouling-cleaning cycle tests.
Comparison of desalination performance between ACQD-TFN membranes and other recently developed membranes in literature.

head.ce:title: Amino-embedded carbon quantum dots incorporated thin-film nanocomposite membrane for desalination by pervaporation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Environment, City University of Hong Kong, Tat Chee Avenue Kowloon, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Innovation Center for Advanced Medical Devices
National Institute of Advanced Medical Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Innovation Center for Advanced Medical Devices, National Institute of Advanced Medical Devices, Shenzhen 518110, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, KU Leuven, Celestijnenlaan 200F, B-3001 Heverlee, Belgium
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, KU Leuven, Celestijnenlaan 200F, B-3001 Heverlee, Belgium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study provides an in-depth investigation on the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in fabricating novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes to address the high demand for highly water-permeable pervaporation (PV) membranes for desalination processes, especially for treating high-salinity water, brine, or wastewater. CQDs have emerged as a green nanofiller for improving membrane performance owing to its excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity, uniform dispersion, and rich functional groups. Herein, we synthesize amino-embedded carbon quantum dots (ACQDs) via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method and employ them in interfacial polymerization to fabricate the ACQD-TFN membrane. Benefiting from the incorporation of ACQDs, the optimized ACQD-TFN membrane exhibited an enhanced water flux of 23.2 kg·m−2·h−1 at 70 °C during the treatment of a 10 wt% NaCl solution, which was 44% higher performance than that of the unmodified thin-film composite membrane. The ACQD-TFN membrane's salt rejection remained at 99.9% even after 96,000 ppm·h chlorine exposure, exhibiting excellent chlorine resistance. Also, the ACQD-TFN membrane's regular surface microstructure and increased hydrophilicity endowed good anti-fouling property, and its flux recovery rate reached 91% after 5 fouling-cleaning cycles. This work provides a feasible strategy for fabricating highly permeable, chlorine-resistant, and anti-fouling PV membranes for desalination applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The depletion of freshwater resources caused by rapid population growth and economic development, and the mounting threats to precious natural freshwater resources, such as climate change and water pollution, have become a major concern for sustainable human life over the recent years [1]. Desalination has been recognized as one of the most promising ways to mitigate freshwater scarcity by tapping into alternative water sources. Among various desalination technologies, membrane-based desalination processes, including reverse osmosis (RO), membrane distillation (MD), and pervaporation (PV), have drawn much attention for their low energy consumption, high separation efficiency, and flexibility in the past few decades [2–4]. Currently, RO is the dominant membrane-based desalination technique, constituting over 50% of desalting capacity worldwide [5]. However, RO has several limitations that leave much to be desired, such as intensive energy input and complicated pre-treatment requirements [6]; polyamide (PA) RO membranes' susceptibility to fouling and the adverse effects of disinfection by chlorination on membrane service life, which results in higher operating costs [7,8]; and unsuitability for treating high salinity water [9].

In the search for an alternative membrane technology which can overcome these limitations, pervaporation (PV) has demonstrated itself as an energy-efficient desalination technology, not only in terms of excellent separation performance in treating high-salinity water but also for its good fouling resistance and stability [4,10]. However, although PV's energy consumption can be minimized to the same level of RO by making use of waste heat or renewable energies (e.g. geothermal, solar energy) [11], the PV desalination process is still far from massive industrialization because of its low water permeability. Conventional polymer-based membranes have exhibited reliable operational stability in industrial applications; however, their low productivity and poor anti-fouling properties and flexibilities still hinder the effectiveness of the PV desalination process. Thus, many researchers have sought to design and fabricate a new generation of PV membranes, which have enhanced desalination capability and satisfactory stability [4,10,12].

The mass transfer in PV membranes follows the solution-diffusion model [13]: 1) the water molecules at the feed side are adsorbed and dissolved into the PV membrane when coming into contact with the hydrophilic surface of the selective layer, and 2) the absorbed water molecules penetrate the membrane to the permeate side through the internal channels inside the dense layer [10]. Wang et al. reported that PV membranes can block up to 99.99% of salts regardless of the thickness of the active layer [10], while many others showed that the reduction in selective layer thickness could increase the permeate flux without losing high salt rejection capability [14–16]. Overall, it is generally believed that a thin-film composite (TFC) membrane will demonstrate high-permeability [16–18].

TFC membranes have been widely studied in relation to RO, ultrafiltration (UF), and nanofiltration (NF) for desalination and wastewater treatment [19–21]. These TFC membranes have featured excellent permeability, high selectivity, and wide pH range tolerance compared to other water-permeable membranes, like homogenous polymer-based membranes. Typically, a TFC membrane consists of a sturdy fabric support, a porous middle layer, and an ultrathin (~500 nm) polyamide layer. This ultra-thin active selective layer can be fabricated via the interfacial polymerization between two chemicals, m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). The resultant polyamide layer can ideally allow the water molecules to permeate while rejecting other solutes, including salt ions. Chaudhri et al. coated polysulfone (PSF) hollow fibers with a PVA layer (~100 nm), and the resultant membrane exhibited a flux of 7.4 kg·m−2·h−1 and a high salt rejection when desalting a 3 wt% NaCl solution [22]. Guided by the idea that thin selective layers could achieve high water flux, Meng et al. fabricated a spray-coated PVA/PTFE membrane, which obtained an ultra-high water flux (143.4 kg·m−2·h−1) [23]. Recently, 2D materials, such as graphene oxide and MXene, have been drawing attention as attractive alternatives for fabricating novel MMMs or self-assembly membranes. Castro has summarized the latest development of MXene-based pervaporation membranes [24]. Ding et al. used maleic acid (MA) to cross-link MXene membranes, achieving a surprisingly high water flux (70 kg·m−2·h−1) that was ascribed to the ultrathin selective layer (~30 nm) [25].

However, there are still some drawbacks impeding the industrial application of TFC membranes in desalination. First is the trade-off between water permeability and selectivity, which is one of the major concerns in membrane development and application. Also, TFC membranes' efficiency could drop significantly when suffering organic, inorganic, and microorganism fouling, while disinfection processes using chlorine could destroy the polyamide layer by deconstructing the amide bonds between MPD and TMC, undermining the membrane's stability.

These practical obstacles have catalyzed a sustained innovation in novel membrane design and the search for new materials. In recent years, membrane research has been geared toward the incorporation of nanotechnology as a way to improve performance and address technical hurdles, and new membranes with superior performance and unprecedented new characters have been developed with the help of nano-sized materials [26–28]. Notably, incorporating hydrophilic nanomaterials has shown great potential as an approach for developing highly permeable membranes [26–32]. One such nanomaterial is carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) or carbon dots (CDs). CQDs are an emerging carbon-based nanomaterial [33–35] with fascinating properties, such as nano-scale sizes (2–10 nm), tunable surface functional groups, inexpensive and facial production methods, and good biocompatibility. These properties make CQDs a promising material in many applications, especially in membrane-based desalination and wastewater treatment [36–43].

As such, a number of studies have attempted to incorporate CQDs in membrane fabrication for desalination and wastewater treatment purposes. Sun et al. incorporated a negatively charged CQDs intermediate layer into the polyamide layer of NF membranes and found that this sandwiched structure can reduce solution transport resistance and mitigate concentration polarization [44]. Li et al. added super hydrophilic CQDs (6.8 nm) into the water phase during interfacial polymerization to fabricate an RO membrane. The membrane's contact angle decreased from 78.6° to 63.2° as the CQDs loading was increased to 0.08 wt%, and the best desalination performance (88.7 kg·m−2·h−1 of water flux and 99% of salt rejection, 2000 ppm NaCl, 1.55 MPa) was achieved with 0.02 wt% CQD content [36]. Bi et al. reported an in-situ interfacial polymerization between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and TMC occurring inside the pores of UF membranes, and benefiting from the pore structure, the membrane exhibited an ultrafast water permeation of 244.7 kg·m−2·h−1·bar−1 [45]. Zhao et al. embedded CQDs (3.2 nm) with polydopamine on the surface of a polyethersulfone (PES) membrane via covalent bonding, and the resulting membrane showed enhanced anti-fouling propensity [46]. By reacting with different chemicals, CQDs can be given various functional groups, opening wider possibilities [41,42,47,48]. Wu's group investigated the effects of CQDs' functional groups by incorporating amino, carboxyl, and sulfonic acid functionalized CQDs (ACQDs, CQDs, SCQDs) into the polyamide layer of nanofiltration membranes. The results showed that the SCQDs-TFN membrane exhibited the highest water flux of 42.1 kg·m−2·h−1 due to its superior hydrophilicity, while the NCQDs-TFN membrane showed a better retention rate to divalent cations because of its positively charged membrane surface [48].

We seek to explore the opportunities CQDs offer in developing reliable and scalable membranes with enhanced separation performance that could overcome the challenges in desalination – specifically, high energy consumption, low water productivity, and fouling and scaling issues [4]. In this study, amino-embedded carbon quantum dots (ACQDs) were synthesized via a facial hydrothermal method with citric acid and polyethyleneimine (PEI). The synthesized ACQDs were added into the aqueous solution during interfacial polymerization to fabricate ACQD-TFN membranes for high-salinity water desalination via PV. The ACQDs created an ultra-thin polyamide layer with a loose structure and enhanced hydrophilicity, and the resultant ACQD-TFN membranes were characterized with various instruments to reveal their physicochemical properties. We further investigated the fabricated membranes by testing their long-term stability, anti-fouling, and chlorine resistance and report that the optimized ACQD-TFN membrane showed an increase in water flux by 44% as well as significantly improved chlorine resistance and anti-fouling performance.

The advantages of being nano-sized and having abundant functional groups as well as good hydrophilicity and biocompatibility make CQDs a promising nanomaterial for developing the new generation of high permeable PV membranes. To the best of our knowledge, few papers have reported the fabrication and application of CQDs-incorporated membranes in the PV desalination field. The findings of this study contribute to addressing the growing demand for an in-depth investigation on the effects of CQDs on the desalination performance, chlorine resistance, and anti-fouling properties of TFN membranes.

Citric acid, humic acid (90%), sodium chloride (NaCl), and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO, 30%) were obtained from Dieckmann (Hong Kong). Polyethyleneimine (PEI, M
W =70,000, 50%) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD, >99%) were purchased from Aladdin. Trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%) was obtained from Bide Co., Ltd. N-hexane was purchased from Anaqua Global International Inc. Ltd. Commercial polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration (UF) membranes with a molecular weight cut-off of 50,000 Da purchased from RisingSun Membrane Technology Co., Ltd. were used as the support. Deionized (DI) water was obtained from a water purification system (Milli-Q, France). All chemicals were AR grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-embedded carbon quantum dots (ACQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: ACQDs were synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal method (Fig. S1) [49]. In brief, 5 g of citric acid and 5 g of PEI were dissolved in 100 mL of DI water. The resultant homogeneous solution was then transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave with a 150 mL Teflon liner and heated at 250 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant brown solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm syringe microfilter to remove impurities. Subsequently, the filtrate was subjected to a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 3 days to remove the unreacted precursors. The remaining solution in the dialysis bag was put into an oven and heated overnight at 90 °C to remove the water. Finally, ACQD powders were obtained after freeze-drying.

The ACQD membranes were fabricated through a typical IP process (as shown in Fig. 1 ). Specifically, MPD was dissolved into DI water to form a 2 wt% aqueous solution, then certain amounts of ACQDs were added (the ratio to MPD was 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 wt%, respectively). Each ACQDs/MPD solution with a different ACQDs loading was processed as follows to fabricate ACQDs polyamide membranes. 30 mL of the well-mixed ACQDs/MPD solution was poured onto the PES substrate (24 cm2) and kept for 10 min. Then, the ACQDs/MPD solution was discarded, and the remaining droplets on the surface of the substrate were removed using compressed air from an airbrush. Subsequently, 10 mL of 0.1 wt% TMC/hexane solution was poured onto the surface and kept for 30 s to carry out the IP reaction. The membrane was rinsed with hexane 3 times and placed into an oven for a 10 min heat treatment at 80 °C. The prepared membranes were denoted as TFC (without ACQDs) and ACQD-TFN membranes, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of ACQDs and fabricated membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical composition of the ACQDs was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Shimadzu IRAffinity-1) with a wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1. The particle size distribution was measured via a dynamic light scattering system (DLS, Zetasizer Nano ZS90). The photochemical properties of the ACQDs were measured by a UV-VIS-NIR spectrometer (UV–vis, Shimadzu UV-3600) and fluorescence spectroscopy (PL, Shimadzu RF-5301PC).

The membrane morphology was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss EVO MA10). The membranes were coated with gold by a sputter coater to enhance their conductivity in the SEM test. The surface chemical composition was revealed by attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Shimadzu IRAffinity-1). Static water contact angle (CA, Kruss DSA 25S) was used to examine the membrane surface hydrophilicity.

The desalination performances of the fabricated ACQD-TFN and TFC membranes were evaluated via a lab-scale flat-sheet vacuum PV setup (as shown in Fig. 2 ). The feed tank was placed on the electric balance connected to a computer to record the feed solution's mass change. The feed solution was pre-heated to a certain temperature (50–90 °C), then transferred to the membrane module by a gear pump at a flow rate of 20 L·h−1. The effective area of the membrane was 19.63 cm2. The vapor from the permeate side was condensed into liquid form through a condenser and finally collected in the collector. The vacuum of the permeate side was maintained at −0.1 MPa. The PV performance in terms of water flux J (kg m−2 h−1) and salt rejection R (%) were calculated as follows:
(1)
J =
M
A ∙ t
(2)
R =
C f
−
C p
C f
× 100 %
where M (kg) is the mass change of feed, A (m2) is the effective area of the membrane, t (h) is the operating time of pervaporation, C
f and C
p represent the salt concentration (which was determined by a conductivity meter) in the feed and permeate tank, respectively.

Chlorine resistance tests were carried out by immersing the ACQD-TFN membranes into a NaClO solution (2000 ppm) for 12 h and 48 h, respectively. There are three stages in the chlorine treatment: 1) original stage, 2) short-time chlorination (12 h), and 3) long-time chlorination (48 h). PV desalination tests were performed at each stage to evaluate the impacts of chlorine treatment on the separation performance. After chlorine treatment, membrane surface morphology was demonstrated by SEM to further understand the effects of chlorine on the membrane.

It is generally believed that the incorporation of nanoparticles into the membrane polymer matrix could enhance the resultant water permeability compared to conventional TFC membranes [28]. However, the TFN membrane suffers from low stability caused by the poor compatibility between inorganic nanoparticles and the polymer-based matrix. The introduction of amino groups improves the hydrophilicity of CQDs and gives them good dispersion properties in the MPD solution [48]. In addition, these amino groups would also react with TMC to achieve high compatibility in the polyamide layer [41]. Hence, amino-embedded CQDs were synthesized and employed to fabricate the ACQD-TFN membranes. The chemical composition of ACQDs was characterized by FTIR (Fig. 3a). The wideband at 3363 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the phenolic C–OH. The peaks at 1049 and 1693 cm−1 are the stretching of CO and CO, respectively. Two bands at 1390 and 1049 cm−1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH, and the peaks at 2941 and 2865 cm−1 represent the vibration of –CH and –CH2 originating from PEI, respectively [49]. The FTIR result indicates that the amino groups were successfully grafted on the ACQDs. The diameter distribution of the synthesized ACQDs was examined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. As shown in Fig. 3b, ACQDs presented a narrow particle size distribution (1–3 nm) with an average diameter of 1.8 nm. Benefiting from the filtration and dialysis in the post treatment, the ACQDs presented an even size distribution, which is conducive to dispersion.

The photochemistry properties of the ACQDs were characterized by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. In Fig. 3c, the absorbance peak at the ultraviolet region (240 nm) is ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic domain in the carbon core of the ACQDs [49]. The peak at 350 nm is related to the electron transition from n to π* of CC [50]. The fluorescence of the ACQDs was investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy. From the PL spectra (Fig. 3d), it can be seen that the ACQDs' emission wavelength was fixed at ~450 nm under different excitation wavelengths (310–410 nm), and the highest PL intensity was detected at 350 nm excitation wavelength. The inset pictures (Fig. 3c) demonstrate the appearances of the ACQD solutions at daylight (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet light (right), which indicate that the ACQDs can easily form a brown homogeneous aqueous solution and emit a blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation owing to their good hydrophilicity and compatibility.

The surface morphologies of the as-prepared TFC and ACQD-TFN membranes were characterized with SEM, which is shown in Fig. 4 . The TFC membrane (Fig. 4a) exhibited the typical morphology of a polyamide active layer, where sub-micron scale nodules and globules were evenly distributed on the membrane surface, which can be ascribed to the fast interfacial polymerization between MPD and TMC [51]. In contrast, the ACQD-TFN membranes had a relatively rough surface with many large nodules, grainy, and convex structures. This is due to the incorporation of ACQDs, which increased the interfacial polymerization process by creating more interfaces for polyamide group formation, thus changing the microstructure of the polyamide layer [47]. However, when further increasing the ACQDs loading, the ACQD-TFN membranes became more smooth (Fig. 4e and f) due to the steric hindrance of the ACQDs, which slows down the migration rate of MPD and changes the microstructure of the TFN membrane [42]. As can be seen in Fig. 4g-i, the ACQD-TFN membranes were composed of three layers: nonwoven fibers at the bottom, the PES layer in the middle, and the ultrathin ACQDs-embedded polyamide layer on the top. The thicknesses of the PES and nonwoven layers are around 110 μm, which provides enough structural stability for the membrane. The PA layer on the top is around 3 μm thick. Increasing the ACQD loading from 0 to 50 wt% increases the thickness from 111.8 to 114.9 μm, showing that the addition of ACQDs leads to significant increases in the PA layer's thickness.

PV is a promising alternative to RO in desalination because of its intrinsic high salt rejection and capability for treating high salinity water. However, the thick and dense polymer layer of traditional polymer-based PV membranes has restricted the membranes' water permeability. Some of the emerging 2D material-based self-assembly membranes (such as graphene oxide, MXene, and COFs) have shown great water permeability, however, they also suffered severe leaching issues [14,21,53]. Moreover, many studies showed the existence of a trade-off between permeability and stability [4,54]. Herein, we managed to construct an ultra-thin active layer with enhanced hydrophilicity and more water channels by incorporating ACQDs into the polyamide layer to overcome the trade-off between flux and robustness. The effect of the ACQD content on desalination performance was evaluated under a crossflow PV experiment at 70 °C. As shown in Fig. 7a, both TFC and ACQD-TFN membranes exhibited high salt rejection over 99.9%, indicating that the polyamide layer was defect-free. The TFC membrane showed the lowest water flux of 16.3 kg·m−2·h−1 due to its lower hydrophilicity and dense polyamide layer. A prompt increase in water flux (21.7 kg·m−2·h−1) was observed as the ACQD content increased to 10 wt%, eventually to record 23.2 kg·m−2·h−1 for 50 wt% ACQD content. The increase in water flux can be attributed to the incorporation of hydrophilic ACQDs into the polyamide layer during the interfacial polymerization process, which enhanced surface hydrophilicity (as shown in Fig. 6) and reduced water transfer resistance by constructing more water channels. Based on the above results, we assessed the optimal ACQD content to be 50 wt%. Unless otherwise specified, the ACQD-TFN membrane in subsequent experiments represents the membrane with an ACQD content of 50 wt% because of its uppermost desalination performance at various feed temperatures and feed concentrations (refer to Fig. S2 and S3).

The effects of feed temperature and concentration are presented in Fig. 7c and Fig. 7d. As the feed temperature increased from 50 to 90 °C, the flux showed an exponential growth (from 8.8 to 46.5 kg·m−2·h−1) due to the vapor pressure increment which follows the Clausius-Clapeyron Eq. [55]. The feed concentration effect was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 7d. The water flux decreased slightly from 25.9 to 23.1 kg·m−2·h−1 as the feed NaCl concentration increased from 0 to 10 wt%. When further increasing the feed NaCl concentration to 15 and 20 wt%, the flux significantly dropped (13.4 and 5.6 kg·m−2·h−1).

In addition, a long-term PV test was applied to validate the stability of the ACQD-TFN membrane, and the result is provided in Fig. 7b. The salt rejection remained over 99.9%, and the flux was stable (average 22.7 kg·m−2·h−1) with only small fluctuations during the 40-h PV operation, indicating the remarkable stability of the ACQD-TFN membrane in the long-term desalination test. The improved desalination performance of the ACQD-TFN membrane benefits from the incorporation of ACQDs. The good hydrophilicity and nano-scale properties of ACQDs are desirable for constructing a highly permeable polyamide layer, and the excellent compatibility of ACQDs ensures the high structural integrity of the active layer. The comparison of the ACQD-TFN membrane between other reported PV membranes is given in Table 1 . The ACQD-TFN membrane showed satisfactory flux and rejection for treating high salinity water, however it still has potential to further improve flux compared to other advanced membranes. The reason may be ascribed to the thick PES substrate used as support, which provided good mechanical strength but also prolonged the water transport path and increased resistance. The optimization of the substrate is also an emergent issue in future work.

Chlorine compounds are widely used as a disinfectant in water treatment, however, TFC membranes are prone to damages in the presence of free chlorine in the feed [63,64]. Herein, we evaluated the chlorine resistance of the ACQD-TFN and TFC membranes by immersing them in a 2000 ppm NaClO solution for a certain period prior to the evaluation of their PV desalination performance. As demonstrated in Fig. 8a, the TFC membrane's salt rejection significantly decreased from 99.9% to 11.6% after 12-h chlorine exposure, however, the ACQD-TFN membrane still maintained high salt rejection (99.9%) even after 48-h chlorine exposure. The poor selectivity of the TFC membrane can be ascribed to the degradation of the polyamide cross-linking network [63]. The sacrificial amino groups on the ACQDs underwent the substitution with chlorine, thus preventing the polyamide network from being oxidized and hydrolyzed [65,66]. According to previous studies, chlorine exposure has two opposite effects on polyamide membranes: 1) N-chlorination could increase membrane hydrophobicity and compactness, leading to decreased flux and increased salt rejection; 2) CN hydrolysis would also occur, increasing hydrophilicity but destroying the amide bonds at the same time, which results in an opposite effect to N-chlorination [67]. As shown in Fig. 8b, the water flux of the ACQD-TFN membrane gradually decreased during the tests due to the rise in NaCl concentration as the water is removed from the feed tank. The water flux of the ACQD-TFN membrane slightly dropped after the first 12-h of chlorine exposure because N-chlorination happened first, increasing the hydrophobicity of the membrane. The water flux of the TFC membrane increased to over 100 kg·m−2·h−1, which is close to the flux of the PES support membrane. The surface oxidation status of the TFC and ACQD-TFN membranes, recorded at 0, 4, 12, 24, and 48-h, after immersion in high concentration chlorine-contained solutions (2000 ppm) are shown in Fig. S6. The TFC membrane (Fig. S6a) oxidized very quickly, the surface changing to a yellow color after 4 h immersion, and after 48 h, the color already changed to brown. This indicates that the MPD molecules were completely chlorinated causing degradation of the polyamide cross-linking network. Compared with the TFC membrane, the ACQD-TFN membrane exhibited excellent chlorine resistance, and the oxidation was noticeably delayed by the presence of ACQDs on the membrane surface.

To further investigate the effects of chlorine exposure on the polyamide membrane, we observed the surface morphology using SEM. As shown in Fig. 9 , some defects were observed on the TFC membrane after 12-h chlorine exposure, indicating that the dense polyamide layer was destroyed because of the hydrolysis oxidation of amide groups, while the ACQD-TFN membrane seemed to be intact after chlorine treatment. Moreover, a lot of salt crystals had formed on the surface of the TFC membrane after 48-h chlorine exposure, while only a few crystals existed on the surface of the ACQD-TFN membrane. In conclusion, the amino groups from ACQD nano-particles served as sacrificial groups to slow down the hydrolysis of the polyamide network, leading to a significant improvement in the ACQD-TFN membrane's chlorine resistance [63,66,68,69].

There exists a high demand for highly permeable PV membranes in the water desalination process. In this work, a novel ACQD-TFN PV membrane was fabricated by incorporating ACQDs into the polyamide layer during interfacial polymerization. The effects of ACQDs on the membrane physicochemical properties were characterized by SEM, CA, and FTIR. The fabricated ACQD-TFN PV membrane's increased hydrophilicity and formation of internal water channels endorsed a 44% increment in water flux, while salt rejection was maintained at 99.9% in PV desalination for treating a highly concentrated saltwater of 10 wt%. The sacrificial amino groups on the ACQDs provided firm protection for the polyamide active layer, thus significantly enhancing our membrane's chlorine resistance. Meanwhile, the ACQD-TFN membrane exhibited good anti-fouling property, recording a 91% water recovery rate compared to 68% shown by the TFC membrane during the organic fouling test. The optimized ACQD-TFN membrane demonstrated excellent PV desalination performance, long-term stability, and high reliability under harsh conditions, demonstrating its high potential for treating high-salinity water desalination, especially RO concentrates and industrial wastewater, while achieving less liquid discharge and reducing the membrane maintenance cost. However, there is still a long way to go before CQDs-TFN membranes could reach the stage of industrial application for desalination and wastewater treatment. The scale-up effects of these membranes need to be studied in future. In the meantime, there is a need for further studies on modifying CQDs to build up multiple functional groups that could help membranes to meet the requirements for more potential applications.

Jiawei Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Wei Jia: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Jiaxin Guo: Resources, Methodology. Noman Khalid Khanzada: Resources, Methodology. Pengrui Jin: Resources, Methodology. Pak Wai Wong: Resources, Methodology. Xinning Zhang: Resources, Methodology. Alicia Kyoungjin An: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Research Grant Council of Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China through General Research Fund (Project No. 11209421) and Theme-based Research Scheme (Project No. [T21-604/19-R]).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2022.115742.
